Re-review (round 2) the REVISED plan for plan-flow goal **G13** (milestone M50). CONFIGURED MODE: judge against the canonical `/cq:plan-review` rubric, RETURN your verdict as a single fenced ```json block, write NOTHING to any ledger.

This is a revision round. The previous review R155 (revise) filed 4 criticisms; the plan was revised to address them. Read R155 (reviews ledger) for the criticisms, then read the CURRENT task fields (T141–T148, tasks ledger) and verify each criticism is resolved:
- [opus-C1] T143 grounding was wrong (updateGateLocked ALREADY logs "scheduler aggregation change" at weighted.go:506/514/526 — a new log would double-log). VERIFY T143 now instructs to EXTEND that existing record with from/engage_threshold_fps/disengage_threshold_fps and forbids a second log line.
- [opus-C2] T143/T147 asserted a non-existent "scheduler aggregation engaged/disengaged" string. VERIFY T143 and T147 acceptance now assert the canonical "scheduler aggregation change" (to=aggregating|collapsed) string.
- [fable-C3] T144 asserted on the T141 log-capturer without a dependsOn edge (M52 an independent root). VERIFY T144 now either adds T141 to dependsOn OR rewords acceptance to the existing startProc combined output (proc.log()) preserving M52 independence.
- [fable-C4] T144 left the partial-link_bandwidth-declaration verdict unspecified. VERIFY T144 now pins the partial case (WARN + gauge=0, guard still checks declared paths).

Also re-confirm no NEW faults were introduced by the revision and the DAG remains acyclic and complete for the Q51–Q55 scope. If all 4 are resolved and nothing new is wrong, return `go-ahead`; otherwise `revise` with the residual criticism.

Emit EXACTLY: `{ "summary": "...", "verdict": "go-ahead"|"revise", "new_questions": [...], "criticism": [...], "defects": [...] }`. End with a `### Session summary` (goal G13, role: plan-reviewer (opus), round 2, verdict).

attachment.content:
- baboon: Baboon data model compiler reference. Covers model syntax, multi-version evolution, code generation, wire-format conversions, database migrations, backward compatibility, and version negotiation. TRIGGER when: designing, changing, or discussing any external API, working with .baboon model files, updating data models, adding new fields or types, bumping model versions, running code generation, implementing conversions, writing database migrations, or discussing API versioning strategy.
- constructive-test-taxonomy: Multi-axis classification of tests (Intention × Encapsulation × Isolation) for evaluating test quality and steering test-writing decisions. Replaces fuzzy Unit/Integration/E2E vocabulary with precise tags and a maintenance-cost heuristic. TRIGGER when: writing new tests, reviewing a test suite, deciding what to test or at what level, discussing mocks vs. real dependencies, classifying tests, or arguing about test coverage or test-strategy tradeoffs.
- dual-tests: Pattern for testing code that touches external systems: write one abstract test suite against an interface, then run it against both the production adapter (real DB/API/FS) and a hand-written in-memory dummy. Prefers manual dummies over auto-generated mocks. TRIGGER when: designing code that depends on a database, HTTP API, message queue, filesystem, or other external system; choosing between mocks, fakes, or dummies; adding integration points; setting up test infrastructure; or when the `constructive-test-taxonomy` skill calls for moving Communication-tier tests toward Blackbox-Atomic.
- environment: Bubblewrap "yolo" sandbox escape workflow (exchange scripts for reaching $HOME, system paths, or files outside the sandbox) plus in-sandbox nix/direnv usage. TRIGGER only when $SMIND_SANDBOXED=1 — i.e. running inside the yolo sandbox and you need to reach something outside it. SKIP entirely when $SMIND_SANDBOXED is unset: you are unsandboxed, so write directly and ignore this skill.
- izumi: Best-practice playbook for the izumi Scala ecosystem — distage (DI), distage-framework (roles/RoleAppMain), distage-testkit (test harness with memoization), distage-framework-docker (test containers), logstage (structured logging), and BIO (bifunctor effect typeclasses). Covers idiomatic bindings, Activation/Axis switching, Lifecycle discipline, plugin layout, and how to apply the `dual-tests` pattern inside distage so one test body runs against both a dummy and a real-DB adapter. TRIGGER when: writing or reviewing Scala code that imports `izumi.distage.*`, `izumi.logstage.*`, `izumi.functional.bio.*`, `distage.*`, `logstage.*`; designing a new distage plugin or Role; wiring a testkit `SpecZIO`/`Spec2`/`Spec1`; configuring `ContainerDef`/`DockerSupportModule`; choosing between `StandardAxis.Repo` or `Scene` tags; or implementing the dual-tests pattern in a distage project. The canonical reference project is https://github.com/7mind/distage-example.
- resilient-ws-ui: Guidelines for building applications that maintain reliable WebSocket connections to a backend and surface connection health clearly to the user. Invoke ONLY when the user explicitly references this skill by name (e.g. "/resilient-ws-ui", "use the resilient-ws-ui skill"). Do NOT auto-invoke based on topic match.
- tass: Target/Actual State Separation (TASS) pattern for reliable state management in event-driven control systems. TRIGGER when: implementing device or entity state management, designing control systems, working with target vs actual state separation, or building event-driven systems with commanded/observed state.
- cq:advance: Advance the WHOLE flow one cycle — chain /cq:investigate:advance, /cq:plan:advance, /cq:implement:advance to quiescence, then report DRAINED / BLOCKED-ON-QUESTIONS / MIXED.
- cq:implement-review: Implement-flow adversarial per-task review rubric + stdout-JSON contract, as a portable promptTemplate for non-Claude harnesses (pi/Codex). Symmetric to cq:plan-review. Judges ONE task's implementation, splits findings into autonomously-fixable criticism[], user-only questions[], and out-of-scope defects[], and emits the verdict as JSON on stdout. Writes NOTHING to the ledger — the orchestrator records the terminal review.
- cq:implement:advance: Advance an implement-flow run one full pass — pick DAG-ready tasks, implement each in an isolated worktree, adversarially review, autonomously fix criticism, register user questions, and merge back in dependency order.
- cq:implement:start: Start an implement-flow run — resolve the target milestones (default: all open ones), validate their task DAG, then hand off to the /cq:implement:advance loop.
- cq:investigate: Start an investigate-flow run — defect intake + bootstrap, then run the /cq:investigate:advance pass inline.
- cq:investigate:advance: Advance an investigate-flow run one research round — read defect state, form/extend the hypothesis tree, dispatch read-only explorers, validate every citation against source, adjudicate node status, and on a confirmed root cause file-and-defer the fix to plan-flow.
- cq:plan: Start a plan-flow goal — create the goal (from free text OR one-or-more idea-ids, one goal per idea), then hand off to the planner for the first clarifying questions.
- cq:plan-review: Shared adversarial plan-review rubric — judge an emitted plan for a goal against the fine-grained / sequenced / testable / grounded / complete rubric, classify findings into new_questions / criticism / defects, and emit a single fenced-json verdict on stdout. The CANONICAL rubric source; Claude's plan-reviewer agent and every non-Claude (Codex/Pi) reviewer judge the same way and emit the same JSON.
- cq:plan:advance: Advance plan-flow goals one full round — a given goal, or (no argument) every unlocked goal — running the planner↔reviewer loop until each needs the user or reaches `planned`.
- cq:plan:follow-up: Add new scope to an EXISTING plan-flow goal — append the follow-up request, re-open the goal, and hand to the planner for a fresh clarifying round.
- cq:planners: Session-only planner-set override — parse a natural-language planner instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_planners/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- cq:reviewers: Session-only reviewer-set override — parse a natural-language reviewer instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_reviewers/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- codex:rescue: Delegate investigation, an explicit fix request, or follow-up rescue work to the Codex rescue subagent
- codex:setup: Check whether the local Codex CLI is ready and optionally toggle the stop-time review gate
- codex:codex-cli-runtime: Internal helper contract for calling the codex-companion runtime from Claude Code
- codex:codex-result-handling: Internal guidance for presenting Codex helper output back to the user
- codex:gpt-5-4-prompting: Internal guidance for composing Codex and GPT-5.4 prompts for coding, review, diagnosis, and research tasks inside the Codex Claude Code plugin
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Both functionally worked — the single flow striped across both paths — but they are hard to operate safely.

1. **Aggregation-engagement observability.** Weighted mode sits silently in primary-only until offered load exceeds `engage_fraction × per_path_capacity_fps`. With the default `per_path_capacity_fps = 10000` (~100+ Mbps/path) aggregation NEVER engages on a low-throughput / CPU-bound edge — the operator sees `policy="weighted"` but single-path behavior and no signal why. Add a metric + log: `wanbond_aggregation_engaged` (bool) and the current EWMA offered-load vs the engage/disengage thresholds, so "configured but inert" is directly visible. (Empirically: had to hand-set `per_path_capacity_fps = 250` to trigger aggregation at all.)
2. **Capacity-sizing safety.** Either auto-derive the default `per_path_capacity_fps` from measured/declared `link_bandwidth`, or warn/fail-loud when `weighted` is selected but capacity is left at a default the link cannot approach (so aggregation is silently dead). Tie the docs' BDP sizing (`install.md §3a`) to a runtime sanity check.
3. **Pacing tradeoff — expose & document.** Measured on/off at the same 8 Mbps offered load:
   - pacing changed the path split — RTT-weighted 71/29 (off) → capacity-capped ~50/50 (on);

Pin it explicitly: partial declaration => WARN + wanbond_weighted_capacity_sane=0, WHILE T142's hard-fail guard STILL independently checks each DECLARED path (a declared path that contradicts still hard-fails at T142; this WARN concerns the UNdeclared paths that cannot be checked). In the unverifiable case the daemon logs ONE actionable startup WARN (message: declare link_bandwidth on ALL paths so capacity can be checked, or verify per_path_capacity_fps against the BDP sizing in install.md 3a — REFERENCE, do not restate, the G2-owned sizing docs). The metrics endpoint exposes a STATIC unlabeled gauge wanbond_weighted_capacity_sane: 1 when verified sane, 0 when unverifiable; the family is ABSENT entirely under non-weighted policy. Plumb the verdict from the loaded config through internal/device to collector registration as a static registered gauge alongside the Source-driven collector (config-derived, NOT per-peer, hence unlabeled and exempt from the labelPeer back-compat rule). Export the metric name as a constant. 212	//     itself goes down mid-dwell, move to best at once rather than stall.

216		if best < 0 {

219			return
220		}

224			s.pending = -1

227		}

229			s.pending = -1
230			return
231		}
232	
233		// best < s.active: a higher-priority path recovered -> debounced failback.
234		if s.pending != best {

1	package sched
2	
3	import (
4		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/telemetry"
5	)
6	
7	// Negative Pick sentinels. Pick returns a NON-NEGATIVE path index when a datagram
8	// should egress, and one of these when it should not. They are distinct so the Send
9	// path can tell a genuine outage (no eligible path) apart from a transient pacer
10	// shedding a frame while every path is healthy — the two must not read the same in
11	// operator logs or the e2e log-grep harness.
12	const (
13		// PickNone means no path is currently eligible (a total outage among this
14		// scheduler's paths). The Bind maps it to "no healthy path".
15		PickNone = -1
16		// PickPaced means eligible paths EXIST but every one is momentarily paced out, so
17		// this frame is shed (dropped) to bound egress and the send backlog. The paths are
18		// healthy — this is deliberate rate limiting, NOT an outage. Only a pacing-enabled
19		// weighted scheduler ever returns it.
20		PickPaced = -2
21	)
22	
23	// FrameClass tags an outbound datagram with the traffic class the pacer must treat
24	// it as (defect D22). The multipath Bind classifies each frame from the inner
25	// WireGuard message type and passes the class to Pick so a pacing scheduler can give
26	// WireGuard control frames (handshake init/response, cookie reply, keepalive) a
27	// different admission policy than bulk data. Pick() has no other frame-type
28	// visibility, so this is the ONLY channel by which frame-type reaches the pacer.
29	type FrameClass uint8
30	
31	const (
32		// ClassData is bulk/opaque data (a WireGuard transport datagram carrying tunnelled
33		// payload). It is fully paced: under a weighted scheduler with pacing enabled it is
34		// subject to the per-path token buckets and is shed (PickPaced) when they are empty.
35		ClassData FrameClass = iota
36		// ClassControl is a WireGuard control frame — handshake initiation/response, cookie
37		// reply, or keepalive. It is pacing-EXEMPT: a pacing scheduler never sheds it for an
38		// empty token bucket and never spends a data token for it, so sustained bulk overload
39		// cannot starve rekey (defect D22). Control frames originate from OUR OWN WireGuard
40		// engine on the send path (an attacker cannot inject them here), and WireGuard's own
41		// state machine bounds their rate, so exempting them from the data pace is safe.
42		ClassControl
43	)
44	
45	// Scheduler is the send-side path-selection policy the multipath Bind consults
46	// for every outbound datagram. It is the extension seam for the send scheduler:
47	// active-backup failover (ActiveBackup, the P1 MVP) is one implementation; the
48	// later weighted / FEC-aware policy (T21) is a DIFFERENT implementation of this
49	// same interface, swapped in at the composition root with no Bind change.
50	type Scheduler interface {
51		// Pick returns the index of the path the next datagram must egress on, in
52		// the path priority order the scheduler was built over (index 0 = preferred
53		// primary). It returns a negative value when no datagram should be sent this
54		// call; the negative value is one of the Pick* sentinels (PickNone for no
55		// eligible path, PickPaced for a healthy-but-paced-out shed), so the caller can
56		// tell a genuine outage apart from deliberate rate limiting. Pick is safe for
57		// concurrent callers on the Bind's send path.
58		//
59		// class is the frame's traffic class (defect D22): a pacing scheduler exempts
60		// ClassControl (WireGuard handshake/keepalive) from the data token buckets so
61		// bulk overload cannot shed control frames and starve rekey, while ClassData is
62		// fully paced. A non-pacing scheduler (active-backup) ignores class.
63		//
64		// Pick MAY be stateful: a weighted/aggregating scheduler advances its
65		// distribution bookkeeping (deficit/round-robin credits, pacing tokens, offered-
66		// load meter) on every call, so ONE Pick consumes ONE frame-selection slot.
67		// Callers that only want the liveness-derived active set refreshed — without
68		// perturbing distribution — MUST call Recompute, not Pick (see the T40 eager-
69		// failover nudge in the multipath Bind).
70		Pick(class FrameClass) int
71	
72		// Recompute refreshes the scheduler's liveness-derived selection state (which
73		// paths are eligible, which is the active primary) from the current PathHealth,
74		// and logs any active-path transition, WITHOUT selecting or consuming a frame
75		// slot: it advances no distribution/pacing/load state. It is the split-out
76		// "recompute the eligible/active set from liveness" half of the old Pick, so the
77		// eager-failover nudge (defect D18/T40) can drive an egress-lull failover recompute
78		// without stealing a weighted-distribution slot the way a spurious Pick would. For
79		// an idempotent scheduler (active-backup) it is exactly Pick without the return;
80		// for a stateful one (weighted) it is strictly the non-consuming subset. It is
81		// safe for concurrent callers and never calls back into the Bind.
82		Recompute()
83	}
84	
85	// PathQuality is the per-path measured-quality source the weighted scheduler reads
86	// to derive distribution weights: the read side of the T13 telemetry Estimate
87	// (RTT/Jitter/Loss), ANALOGOUS to PathHealth but exposing the quality snapshot
88	// rather than the up/down verdict. *telemetry.Prober satisfies BOTH PathHealth and
89	// PathQuality (its Estimate() is mutex-guarded, so it meets the same concurrency
90	// contract PathHealth documents), which is how one *Prober per path feeds liveness
91	// AND weight to the scheduler. The weighted scheduler's unit tests drive a synthetic
92	// PathQuality so the RTT/loss → weight formula is exercised on hand-built Estimates
93	// with no real probe stream.
94	type PathQuality interface {
95		Estimate() telemetry.Estimate
96	}
97	
98	// DynamicScheduler is a Scheduler whose path set can change at runtime (T30): a
99	// path may be admitted or dropped WITHOUT rebuilding the scheduler or disturbing
100	// the active selection of the surviving paths. The multipath Bind holds its
101	// scheduler as a plain Scheduler and type-asserts to this richer interface only
102	// when a runtime path add/remove is requested, so the Scheduler seam (and the
103	// T21 weighted policy that will implement it) is preserved: a scheduler that
104	// does not support dynamic membership simply cannot back a reloadable bind.
105	//
106	// The index space is the SAME priority-ordered index Pick returns, and the Bind
107	// keeps its own path slice index-aligned with this scheduler's path list, so an
108	// index returned by Pick always addresses the matching path. Both methods are
109	// safe for concurrent callers.
110	type DynamicScheduler interface {
111		Scheduler
112		// AddPath admits h as a NEW lowest-priority path (highest index) and returns
113		// that index. Appending at the tail cannot change any existing path's index
114		// nor steal the active selection from a higher-priority survivor: an
115		// active-backup policy only ever selects the new path once every
116		// higher-priority path is down. h must be non-nil.
117		AddPath(h PathHealth) (int, error)
118		// RemovePath drops the path at index i (shifting higher indices down by one)
119		// and repairs the cached selection so a surviving path keeps being selected
120		// and, if the dropped path was the active one, egress fails over to the best
121		// remaining path on the next Pick. i must be in range.
122		RemovePath(i int) error
123		// SetPaths replaces the ENTIRE path/health list with health (priority order,
124		// index 0 = preferred primary) and clears any cached selection, so the next
125		// Pick re-derives the active path from live liveness. The Bind calls it on
126		// every Open to re-align the scheduler's membership with the path slice it
127		// rebuilds from the current definitions — the single reconciliation point that
128		// makes a runtime path add/remove survive a Close→Open cycle index-aligned
129		// (T30). It is safe for concurrent callers; health must be non-empty and hold
130		// no nil element.
131		SetPaths(health []PathHealth) error
132	}
133	
134	// PathHealth is the per-path liveness the scheduler consumes: the read side of
135	// the T13 telemetry liveness state machine, so the scheduler reads exactly the
136	// up/down verdict T13 already computes (with its own probe-loss hysteresis)
137	// rather than duplicating detection here.
138	//
139	// Concurrency contract: Pick runs on the Bind send path CONCURRENTLY with the
140	// probe/timer goroutines that mutate liveness, so a production PathHealth MUST
141	// be INTERNALLY SYNCHRONIZED. *telemetry.Prober satisfies this — its State()
142	// takes the Prober's mutex (probe.go). A bare *telemetry.Liveness does NOT: it
143	// is not independently synchronized (probe.go: "must be reached only through
144	// the Prober") and its State() is an unguarded field read (liveness.go), so
145	// wiring a live, probe-driven *Liveness directly here is a data race — use a
146	// *Prober or an explicitly-locked adapter instead. (The sched unit tests drive
147	// a raw *Liveness on a single goroutine with no concurrent writer, which is not
148	// a race and is fine for testing.)
149	type PathHealth interface {
150		State() telemetry.PathState
151	}
152	
153	// AlwaysUp is a PathHealth that reports StateUp unconditionally. It is the
154	// composition-root placeholder used until the probe-transport wiring (a
155	// follow-up task) drives real per-path *telemetry.Prober liveness: with every
156	// path statically Up, the active-backup scheduler keeps all egress on the
157	// preferred primary (the data-thrift bring-up behaviour), and swapping in real
158	// Probers later activates failover with no further Bind or scheduler change.
159	type AlwaysUp struct{}
160	
161	// State reports the path as up.
162	func (AlwaysUp) State() telemetry.PathState { return telemetry.StateUp }
